candidates.stmt: I’ve represented Barton & Sandhills on the City Council for the past 15 years and I’ve always done my best for the community, including getting funding for the new library and sports pavilion and to save the leisure centre from potential closure after Covid, and making sure there are affordable homes for local people on Barton Park. I’m out and about in the area day in, day out, having the conversations that show what a strong community ours is, even as we fight to get what we deserve. I fight to get Barton & Sandhills their fair share, to support local people who are my 1st priority.

I’m honoured to be your Reform UK candidate for Wallingford in the Oxfordshire County Council election on May 1st, 2025.

I genuinely believe that the best way to represent a community is to truly understand it.

I’ve been a proud resident and hardworking member of this community for all my life, and I’ve grown to deeply appreciate its unique charm and beauty.

With your support on May 1st, I’ll be dedicated to working tirelessly for you and striving to make Wallingford an even better place to live and work. We’ll protect our stunning countryside and the environment, ensuring that future generations can enjoy the same natural wonders we do.

Tariq Saeed (any pronouns) is a born-and-raised 'Bartoner' who grew up in Barton before studying at the University of Oxford, which they graduated from in 2025 — as a student activist he campaigned for ethnic minority, LGBTQ+, and working-class students.

Tariq is passionate about addressing the challenges facing the environment and understands that our solutions must also benefit everyday working people - he believes “only a party not funded by billionaires and fossil fuel giants can reliably stand up for people AND the planet.”

Born and bred here in Oxford, raising two young children, and fighting for our community every day.

I’ve served as a City Councillor for over 7  years and as Cabinet Member for a Safer Oxford for over 2 years, tackling the issues that matter most to local families.

I’ve delivered safer streets, pushed for better housing and green spaces, and fought crime and anti-social behaviour alongside Thames Valley Police.

Blackbird Leys deserves respect, not to be left behind.

If you want a councillor who shows up, stands up and gets things done, I’m asking for your vote on 7th May.

Blackbird Leys first. Every street matters. Every family counts.

Keep in touch

In Blackbird Leys, families are doing everything right working, raising children, contributing to our community. But too many are still falling behind by rising bills, rising rents and services stretched.

Please get in touch if there’s anything I can help with: lubna.labour@gmail.com.

Indigo Haynes (she/her) is a modern languages student in her final year at Oxford University, with plans to start a teacher training course in Oxford after graduating.

She's been involved with local politics since she was a teenager, having run campaigns in her hometown to save a local hospital from shutdown, get better bus routes with cheaper fares, and led climate change protests and 'Fridays For Future' school walk-outs.

She was also a member of Youth Parliament, and secretary of her local Labour Party; since Starmer's election as Prime Minister, she left the party, feeling betrayed by his government's actions. Now in Oxford, her focus is on the same issues she's always fought for: preserving local services, improving public transport, and working to fix the climate crisis.

Sushila Dhall (she/her) has lived in Jericho and Carfax for over 40 years and during this time has represented local residents on both the County and City Councils, working for equality, human rights and justice.

She works for a charity with refugees and asylum seekers and champions the diversity that makes Oxford such a great city. She is a trade union member and local transport activist, and chair of Oxford Pedestrian’s association. Sushila says, 'Now is the time to stand up for humanity and our beautiful planet.'

Living in Jericho for over 30 years, my children have grown up and went to school here, in the heart of our city. I’m proud to be our local councillor, and I want to keep making a difference for local people. I teach at Oxford Brookes, supporting students and apprentices as they take their degrees in city planning. Most are part time, studying at the same time as working to build their careers. I also chair the Cripley Meadow Allotment Association, where so many residents take great pleasure in gardening and community participation. In this I am committed to my community and supporting others. That’s why I am an active councillor who regularly speaks to hundreds of our neighbours, hearing and championing issues as they arise. If re-elected I will always be a community voice who residents can speak to.

As the city councillor for the Wood Farm and Churchill area for 25 years, and council leader since 2018 I have always worked to secure more genuinely affordable, council, and shared ownership housing in our city, and ensure that our city’s economic growth benefits everyone. I have played a key role in the rollout of the Oxford Living Wage across the City, with 190 local employers paying £14.06 an hour from April 2026, and have local businesses and jobs. As well as my duties I work part-time at Oxford University Hospitals and have two daughters born at the JR and attended local schools. This local link has driven me to help secure the future of youth provision in Wood Farm, support local community groups, repave the path through Magdalen Woods, and crack down on antisocial behaviour, with new lighting and my monthly litterpick. I care deeply for the residents and am dedicated to supporting them year-round.

Ines Wilhelm (she/her) has lived in Oxford for 15 years. Her love for the city and its people inspired her to build a life here and to join local grassroots initiatives, from liveability, road safety and cycling groups to community volunteering. Growing up with a single mother who worked two nursing jobs, she learned the importance of mutual support and the realities of making ends meet that many Oxford families face.

Ines believes deeply in Oxford’s potential to become a fairer, more welcoming, and greener city. With more Green Party councillors in the city than ever, she is keen to address the stark inequalities in our city, and to champion the protection and enhancement of Oxford’s green spaces, biodiversity, and climate resilience.

I've been an Oxford resident for 10 years, having lived in both Rose Hill and Iffley Fields. I am a regular cyclist (hence the photo)!, and a bus user and very rarely drive owing to a mild disability.

I am campaigning on five issues:

1. Stopping the LTN’s / Traffic filters to allow more bike / bus provision and parking. - Much prefer better cycling / bus provision is key here - e.g. Bike racks, Bike and Bus shelters (have an ongoing wager that there isn't a single public bike shelter in Littlemore and Rose Hill), and removing potholes (have reported around 250 over the years). Stopping / removing LTN's (specifically the Crowell road one on the way to Temple Cowley) frees up a lot of funding for better ideas. I'm passionate on tackling the climate emergency, but we shouldn't do this by punishing motorists - let's make Oxford a lovely place for ALL road users.

2. More local amenities -  Specifically a new GP’s surgery for Littlemore (which helps Rose Hill too). With the new developments going on, there should be better amenities and infrastructure put in FIRST (e.g. with the new houses behind the George Inn, and the Railway lane development).

3. More / Better street lighting -  More of an issue in Littlemore, but currently the lights are not bright enough and they could be more efficient (e.g. through better LED's).

4. Better bin services -  Specifically higher recycling rates - Oxford City is amongst the lowest in Oxfordshire and I'm passionate about changing that. Making the Green bin the recycling bin may help that, but I also want to educate Houses of Multiple Occupation better on using them. Oh, and some more public recycling bins around Rose Hill shops and Littlemore generally wouldn't go amiss. With people going across to the Kassam stadium: sometimes there is litter, and we can improve that by providing more recycling bins.

5. Council tax freeze (county and city) -  City council tax is MORE THAN DOUBLE in Oxford than Witney and Henley. I'm the only candi

I am a retired surveyor who has a keen interest in housing matters and in particular the shortage of social housing in Oxford.
I have lived in East Oxford for over 30 years. I spent 10 of those years on the Committee of Divinity Road Area Residents’ Association in various roles including that of Chair. I worked hard during that period to promote the interests of local residents with a particular focus on traffic and planning issues. I was part of a team that was influential in preventing developments that were harmful to
the local environment.
As a bird lover I am concerned about the quality of the natural environment in Oxford and will campaign to protect and enhance it.
I am a keen cyclist and support the Strategic Cycle Network for Oxfordshire.

Edward Mundy (he/him) has been an Oxford City Councillor since 2021, then standing for the Labour Party. He was an officer in the Labour Group, leader of the Community Independents, and spent 4 years chairing the General Purposes Licensing Committee.

Leaving Labour in late 2023, in response to their leadership failing to condemn the catastrophic IDF assault on Gazan civilians, Edward joined the Green Party at the beginning of this year. This is a year of huge potential for our party both locally and nationally, as was shown in Gorton & Denton.

Edward has a trade unionist background, having represented postal workers across Oxfordshire during one of the most bitter postal strikes in decades. He now helps local people in OX4 as an adviser, so he is acutely aware of the issues that are impacting families in Cowley during the cost of living crisis.

I'm proud to call Oxford my lifelong home. Born and raised here, l've always been deeply committed to our community and have worked hard to make it a better place for everyone.

Whether it's supporting local organisations or helping young people thrive, l've always believed in building a stronger, happier Oxford.

In 2021, I was elected as a city councillor with the aim of making a real difference for residents. During this time, I saw the introduction of Low Traffic Neighbourhoods (LTNs). While I initially waited to see the evidence, it quickly became clear to me - and the vast majority of residents - that these schemes don't work. Instead, they've increased congestion and caused frustration across our city. I've spoken out against them because I believe policies that fail to serve our community should be abandoned.

After leaving the Labour Party, I continued to represent the people of Lye Valley. Whether it was resolving an elderly resident's 9-month battle with Thames Water or transforming the long-unused café at Shotover View into the thriving Waste2Taste community hub, l've always worked to solve the issues that matter most to you.

I'm now standing as a candidate for the Independent Oxford Alliance in the May 2025 County Council elections. This group stands for truly independent representation - free from party politics - so I can focus entirely on what's best for our community. Together, we can fight for our neighbourhoods, protect our streets, and create a brighter future for our children.

This is our city, our home, and our chance to stand together. It's now or never.

I have lived in Oxford for 46 years. I run a language school in the city centre, and was a school governor for some years. Before that I was a teacher, and publisher of educational textbooks. We have good schools in the Cowley area, and large employers in the Mini plant and NHS trusts. As your councillor, I would seek to support the work of the council to boost employment, training and apprenticeship opportunities, and to get the best out of local services for our community. We have an immediate need to attend to issues with roads, travel and buses, but beyond that in an uncertain world I would support projects and work which brings our communities together.

Andrea Schiavi (he/him) moved to Oxford in 2010 and has called it home ever since, drawn by its strong, diverse communities.

Andrea is standing as a local councillor because they care deeply about equality, fairness and ensuring everyone can benefit from what Oxford has to offer. They believe more must be done locally to improve access to housing, services and opportunity, especially as economic pressures affect some communities more than others.

A scientist by training, Andrea now works in tech and volunteers on the board of a local bike charity. They are committed to bold local action on the climate crisis, from sustainable transport to greener neighbourhoods, and to keeping Oxford ambitious while putting people at the centre of decision‑making.

Andrea says, “The fight for the climate is the fight for a better life for our community, right now

Nancy Cartwright is a Professor in philosophy and the history of science, especially physics and economics. Recently her work has focused on how to use scientific research to shape better policy making, including with regard to education, child protection, and international development.

Nancy has worked in many Universities across Britain and beyond, receiving much recognition for her work.

Rosie (she/her) has lived in Oxford for almost 10 years and was first elected as a Councillor in 2022.

She first moved to the city to work for Oxfam where she worked on campaigns to end British arms sales to Saudia Arabia being used in Yemen, against corporate tax evasion, and for refugee family reunion. She now works for a national charity, supporting radical campaigns for climate and migrant justice.

Rosie has a background in the trade union movement and has supported university staff in campaigns for fairer pensions, worked alongside public sector unions in the anti-austerity movement and supported gig economy workers to fight for fair pay and conditions. She also recently completed a PhD researching the impacts of campaigns to improve pay and conditions in the global electronics industry.

I am a local campaigner and activist with strong family links to Donnington. I am committed to getting the best for local residents, and believe in the importance of working with trade union and community groups. I currently work for a community energy company and am proud to be supporting a city council in Oxford which is dedicated to climate and nature. I believe voters should support the Labour party on Oxford City council to choose a bold group which is delivering for the needs of working people in Donnington and across the city.

Dr Graham Jones lives at 45 James Street, off Cowley Road in East Oxford. After an award-winning first career in journalism, he entered academia in 1996 and is a senior research associate attached to Oxford University School of Geography. Liberal Democrat City Councillor for St Clements Ward 2010 to 2014, he served as Opposition Lead on Sport and Leisure, and then Environment and Public Health, and as city nominee on the Asian Cultural Centre committee. He is Chair of Governors at East Oxford Primary School, a Trustee of the City of Oxford Charity (serving on the committee managing Stones Court Almshouses in St Clements), and a Trustee of East Oxford Community Centre. He chairs Oxfordshire Region branch of the European Movement, which campaigns for the UK to stay a full member of the European Union. Married to a speech and language therapist, he has four grandchildren.

I’ve been enormously proud to serve Headington and Oxford as a whole these past years. Serving as Councillor has given me the chance to ensure all communities are heard in City Hall, that our heritage is protected, and opportunity is fostered for a new generation needing an affordable place to live and a city that works. Above all, we need councillors that stay in touch all year round, that meet with and speak with residents every week and every day. But there’s much more to do. If re-elected, I will continue to champion our area and all residents, whatever your politics.

I am proud to have been born, schooled and work in and around Headington, which I am proud to call my home. I work for the OXSRAD charity where I am a supervisor coordinating inclusive activities for disabled people. I cherish the opportunity to support vulnerable people and to create a more welcoming community in Oxford. I am also a Domestic Abuse Champion for the charity Reducing the Risk, working to raise awareness of domestic abuse and ensure that vulnerable women are supported and safeguarded. I am committed to public service and my lifelong connection to Headington means I understand the needs, strengths, and concerns of local residents, and I am committed to representing our community with compassion, dedication, and integrity.

Neil Doig is a Geography teacher in Oxford, a sustainability lead, and the founder of Money Tipps, an education platform helping people build financial confidence. He is also the author of two books that make complex ideas simple and practical for everyday life.

Working across Oxford’s schools and communities, Neil has seen first-hand the challenges people face, from the cost of living to the impact of climate change. With experience in finance, energy, and education, he brings a practical, level-headed approach focused on real results.

Neil is standing for the Green Party to deliver cleaner air, safer cycling and walking routes, more affordable housing, and a stronger local economy, offering trusted, responsible leadership that puts people first.

As a councillor for Headington Hill & Northway I’ve been working for you to improve the area.

I’m passionate about our community and the people who live here. For Plowmans residents I’ve secured a new bus stop and fought for quicker repairs to the tower. I’ve got the council to begin looking after the ponds by the community centre. I’ve got the alleyway at crotch crescent repaired and lighting installed. I’m proud of the Northway estate, i fight for policies like free bulky waste and more grass verge cuts because of this.

As a young person living in Oxford, I’m very conscious of the cost of renting, and hope to continue to fight for affordable housing.

I have also secured extra community wardens to tackle antisocial behaviour, and I continue to push for employers to pay the Oxford living wage. Cost of living in our city is already high enough, and i oppose the inequitable congestion charge, which unfairly punishes many residents.

I have lived in Oxford since I was four, first in North Oxford then in East Oxford. I attended the excellent Cherwell School. I have raised four children, who attended schools in East and North Oxford. I am a proud Oxonian, appreciating the architecture and varied options for art and culture. I consider myself a sort of “Godmother for Oxford” promoting our great City to visitors.

After completing an MPhil in Physiology, I trained as a chartered accountant, and now work as a tax adviser. I also run a small property business. I have developed many transferable skills that would benefit work as a County councillor. For example, scrutinising financial reports and forecasting.

Since moving to Marston eight years ago, I have noticed a steady and deeply troubling deterioration in basic amenities for Oxford residents. Both councils appear to happily sign off to spend millions to fix things that are not broken, and put in place damaging schemes. Yet there is no money to do basic things - maintain our roads, clean our streets, cut the grass, pick up autumn leaves. Both councils are comprehensively failing in their duty of care or any service level agreement

I can no longer stand by and allow the incumbents to damage our city further. Hence I am standing as your independent candidate in the Marston & Northway Division. If I am fortunate to be elected, I will work to remove the LTNs that have damaged the lives of so many residents and businesses. I will stop the impending traffic filters and bus gates. I will do all I can to get the failing County departments to fulfil their duty of care to residents and businesses in Marston & Northway.

Sam (he/him) works as an energy advisor helping residents try to cope with predatory energy firms and access the technology that can help reduce them.

He has been a committed housing activist serving as treasurer of ACORN Oxford, campaigning to prevent unjust evictions and hold bad landlords to account. He volunteers with Oxford Mutual Aid and various local groups including Oxford Synagogue and Community Land Trust.

In these roles see the inequality and vulnerability plaguing this city, driven by deep structural causes. Sam says: “A Green led administration is a chance to turn the power of Oxford City council against the causes of inequality, and make it a better place for us all.”

I have lived in Hinksey Park ward for over 35 years. I would communicate and champion the views and interests of Hinksey Park residents from a position of deep local knowledge. I live here. I rely on our buses and local services. I care about its history and its future.

I have worked in the public sector for decades: for the NHS in a child and adolescent psychiatric unit; and for 35 years as a teacher in comprehensive schools in Bicester and Wallingford. I led a large English department, and was a union rep supporting workers’ rights and conditions in constructive dialogue with management.
I have spent my retirement working in our local community, including being out on the doorstep most weekends listening to people’s concerns. I would be an active councillor, responding promptly to provide the help and action needed. Hinksey Park must be kept safe and welcoming for people of all ages and backgrounds – those that have lived here for many years and those people who want to make their futures here.

Hannah (she/her) is the head of a not-for-profit business network supporting the growth of climate tech startups across Oxfordshire and the South East. Before this, she worked for a sustainability charity, working with key stakeholders across Oxfordshire to accelerate the transition to a greener, smarter, more equitable county.

She is a tireless advocate of climate activism and action, having won the Oxfordshire Climate Action Champion award in 2024. She also co-founded the county-wide campaign Switch Up Your Lunch, encouraging uptake of healthier, greener food choices.Hannah has lived and/or worked in the Hinksey park area for over five years, and is familiar with the local issues it faces - she’s currently involved in Grandpont’s anti-fascism community group.

Hannah says, “The time is now for bold, community-centred action, making a better place for people and planet.”

Hinksey Park residents since 1993, I know and love this ward. I care deeply for the residents and their concerns.

In my professional life I run a department of 80 people that generates £5M income per year. I sit on national and international committees and am very used to public speaking. I have the skills to be an effective councillor, influencing people and.getting the deep.understanding of the issues.

The ward has changed enormously in my time living here. Traffic congestion, house prices and flooding have all got worse. We own our own home, but I am aware how hard it is for first time buyers. I hate losing green space as much as anyone, but feel we must develop Redbridge Meadow for housing. I have reservations about aspects of the proposal, and would work hard to improve the plans if elected.

However, building on Bertie Park would add few houses for the loss of a much loved play area. I pass through there regularly and see the kids having fun.

There are brown field sites that should be developed. More houses should be in the proposals for Oxpens and Osney Mead, and also the unused garage space in New Hinksey.

It's great to see the Kelpie Cafe opening on Abingdon Road. We need to do more to help the businesses on what should be a vibrant street.

Whilst we do need better pedestrian and cycle access from Grandpont Nature Reserve to the city, the proposed bridge is all wrong. It takes out so many trees, when access from the old Gasworks bridge could easily be achieved with minimal environmental impact.

I strongly support the South Oxford Welcomes initiative to counter the aggressive imposition of flags, which is intended to both make members of our community feel threatened, and to challenge our inclusive values.

Congestion: all the data show that the traffic filters have reduced traffic, boosted bus services and improved air quality. Not by much yet, because people are still wedded to their cars.

If elected I will do my best for the community.

Alfie Davis is the co-director of Oxford’s largest food bank, and former student activist and campaigner, having graduated in 2024. They currently live in Jericho, and joined the Green Party in 2025.

As a student, they campaigned for working class students, accessible/affordable accommodation and housing, as well as Palestinian and LGBTQ+ liberation - co-founding Oxford Trans Pride in 2023 and was awarded LGBTQ+ Undergrad of the Year in 2024.

“The widespread poverty in Oxford that I witness every day through my work, and the lack of substantial action being taken by governments angers me - and is what motivates me to stand” says Alfie.

Hi! I’m Awab Kennedy, a student at Teddy Hall! That means that I understand what it is that students need most: better quality housing that doesn’t cost a fortune, or that you have to queue for overnight, a more vibrant nighttime economy, and real action on the climate crisis. That’s why I’m standing to be your next city councillor, to ensure that you have a real voice on the things that matter most to you. If that’s something that appeals to you, then make sure to vote Labour on the 7th of May!

Since 2018, I’ve been proud to stand up for Littlemore — taking action, solving problems and empowering our community. From supporting more than seven families at risk of homelessness, to securing progress on Denny Gardens allotments, backing our shops and green spaces, and championing the Future Leaders of Littlemore Youth Council, I work hard because I believe in this place. I’ve pushed for safer streets, better transport, stronger policing, and real accountability for housing providers. When residents raise concerns, I act — whether it’s broken lifts, parking pressures, anti‑social behaviour or dangerous roads. Littlemore became my community when I moved here from Portugal to work in the local car industry, my daughter was born in John Radcliffe Hospital . I’m proud of what we’ve achieved together — and I’m ready to keep delivering for you and Littlemore welcoming for all.

Tamsin Blaxter (she/her) is a researcher of food system sustainability. Her work focuses on how we can have better conversations about contentious food issues, and the values, assumptions and habits that drive polarisation in our society.

She has lived in Littlemore for four years, during which time she has been a Girlguiding leader and volunteered with the Greens.

Before this she spent ten years living in Cambridge where she had a long history of queer and trans activism, including setting up a long-running series of events for LGBTQ+ History Month, and being part of the committee that created and ran the first ever Cambridge Pride.

She is quickly developing roots here, and cares deeply about making our community safe, green, and welcoming.

James Thorniley (he/him) is a local campaigner, parent and school governor who has lived with his young family in Lye Valley ward for eight years. He is a software engineer for an independent online radio and music platform.

James Thorniley said: "The Labour Party has let down local people in Lye Valley. I'm committed to representing all residents here, and making Oxford fairer, greener and more equal. As the second largest party on Oxford City Council, Greens are best placed to hold the Labour council to account."

I was born in Oxford and live in the Lye Valley area. I work for Oxford University, where I bring research income to our city and promote projects that create local impact that actually makes a difference to communities. I want everyone in Lye Valley to have access to the best opportunities in our city. This means better roads, better buses, and opposing traffic controls.

I want us to be proud of the community we live in, filling the potholes, and clearing fly-tipping off our streets. And I want to regulate overcrowded and unloved HMOs. As a local resident, I see and experience these issues daily and want the best for my neighbours.

Kate Robinson (she/her) has lived in the Marston area for over 20 years and has been in the Green Party for over 15 years. She was elected in 2024, following the good work of Marston’s first Green Cllr, Alistair Morris. Kate is Co-founder of Marston Community Growing, a member of Low Carbon Marston, Marston Eco Group and a Governor at St Michael’s Primary School.

Kate is currently a public librarian, working at Headington Library, and used to be a full-time teacher in secondary schools. She is passionate about community and environmental action, empowerment and education – and works hard to represent all residents and make their voices heard.

I love getting out and speaking to residents where I live in Marston, hearing residents’ concerns and engaging with my community through local events. As a Parish Councillor and Chair of Mortimer Hall I am always keen to hear your feedback on our community, and have always pushed for improvements in our area, such as better cycling infrastructure and directed residents to key services. As your City Councillor I would build on this record, working closely with Cllr Mary Clarkson and Cllr Mark Lygo to represent our community and deliver on key priorities. As a Labour candidate I am opposed to the congestion charge, the workplace parking levy, and want to make sure our community is kept tidy – especially through cutting grass verges and reducing litter.

I am committed to building a stronger Leys community by working hard for better public services and opportunities for all residents.

The best thing about growing up on the Leys are the people.  I grew up on the Blackbird Leys and went to school in Redefield.  My friends and family live on the estate.

I was a youth worker at the Blackbird Leys youth centre for many years and enjoy bringing people together through music.  I have seen neighbours come together to support families in need, and young people express their creativity through music events at the youth centre and venues.

In the 1990s I organised local musicians and volunteers, secured funding, resulting in over £5,000 raised which established a live music project that ran for many years and I wrote a music proposal which later evolved in  Soundwork Studios providing ongoing music education and creative opportunities for hundreds of young people in our community. I feel passionately that our young people should have even more opportunities – not just in music but good jobs, homes and a future.

There are generations of people who make the Leys the good place that it is to live.  My vision is to build on the compassion and inclusiveness of Blackbird Leys by working hard to improve public services for everyone and a vibrant new community centre.

Having grown up on the estate and worked as a youth worker at Blackbird Leys youth centre, I have seen firsthand the talent and potential in our community. That’s why Ibelieve we need better housing, roads, jobs, and healthcare. As your representative, I will ensure residents’ voices are heard at City Council and that residents get the public services they deserve

Join me in building a brighter future for the Leys – where every resident has access to quality housing, jobs, healthcare, and opportunities to thrive.

David Newman (he/him) lives in Greater Leys, where he is a Blackbird Leys parish councillor and chair of the group preparing a neighbourhood plan.

He is keen to see the Leys area develop, and passionate about protecting our great green spaces and fighting the cost of living crisis by reducing energy costs.

My name is Paul Sims and I live in Greater Leys. I have lived in Greater Leys for almost 23 years now, having been a resident since the estate was built in 1995. I chose to live in Oxford as it is a place I have always loved, having first come here in the early 1980s. I have worked in the entertainment industry all my life and I love it, its likely many have seen me in and about Oxford at various venues either DJing or running Karaoke nights. My wife was born and raised in Oxfordshire and is supportive of my wish to become a councilor so as to be able to help our local community.

Labour see Blackbird Leys as a safe seat and have done little for the community. Show them Blackbird Leys matters and vote for Paul Sims. Someone who will champion local issues and causes.

MAKE YOUR VOTE COUNT.

Bullet Points:

1. Ensuring Roads Are Repaired And Maintained.
We all know how bad the roads get in Blackbird Leys. I will push for action to be taken to repair and maintain the roads making them safe for drivers and cyclists.

2. Tackling Crime.
I know people are concerned about drug dealing in areas where there are children. I am against vigilanty approaches to this as I think it makes the situation more confrontational. I will however work with the police to make areas such as parks, schools, community centers and shopping areas no go places for drug dealers. That way parents can feel safe letting their children go to such places. I will also work with the police to help deal with other crimes as well.

3.Anti Social Behavior.
Anti social behavior affects everyone and can ruin communities. I want to help tackle this within the council and also with the local police. I want to make people responsible for their actions. Oxford has a housing crisis and if people want to be anti social then I believe they do not want to be part of the community. There are plenty of people wanting homes who would appreciate having a home and not be anti social. I feel anti social beha

Rosemary Morlin has lived in Northfield Brook since 2001 and is a freelance editor. She has been active in the Leys Residents’ Association and has sat on Blackbird Leys Parish Council as well as being the founder and co-ordinator of the Mole Place Neighbourhood Watch.

Lois Muddiman (she/her) has lived in Osney and St Thomas for over 30 years and was elected as its first Green Party City Councillor in 2022. She was Chair of West Oxford Community Association for ten years and co-founder of Low Carbon West Oxford, Hogacre Common and West Oxford Community Renewables.

She has previously worked in education and community development and is now a practicing artist. As a City Councillor, Lois is Deputy Leader of the Oxford Green Group, Chair of the General Purpose Licensing Committee and works hard to support local residents.

I have lived in Osney and St Thomas since 2022 with four lovely housemates and a somewhat challenged cat. In this time, I have seen repeated problems that have frustrated me — from the Botley Road closure fiasco, to Thames Water’s ongoing delays, to the state of our main roads and public realm. Too often we are told these issues are the result of limited resources, market forces or ageing infrastructure. I don’t agree. I believe many of these problems stem from structural weaknesses in how local government is organised and empowered. It is a matter of organisation and political will. My experience as a local government officer has shown me where the system falls short, and I am standing to help fix it at its roots.

Hi, I’m Harry and I am in my second year of studying at Pembroke College in the Osney and St Thomas ward. I feel passionately about giving back to this community, by fighting for better housing, a healthier environment and improved transport connections. I have recently led the Oxford Students Liberal Association so I believe strongly that it is only the Lib Dem’s that have the compassionate, sensible and principled policies to solve the problems that our society faces.

As your councillor for the past 11 years I have served Quarry and Risinghurst, living and working locally for a charity as a qualified accountant. I have been active through the Headington and Risinghurst Community Associations and serve as Oxford City Council’s Cabinet Member for A Health and Fairer Oxford and as well as the council’s Small Business Champion. I have previously served as a governor at The Swan School and oversaw Oxford’s 28 council allotments. I believe in the transformative record of Oxford’s Labour council and how it has positively served my community in Quarry and Risinghurst, from delivering new, affordable and council housing, to leisure and parks, while staying finnancially buoyant in challenging times.

**Getting things done for a safer, greener community**

Dr Kai Zolleis is running this May to join Roz Smith on the City Council. Kai is a dedicated civic volunteer, previously having contributed as a school governor and PTA chair at Windmill Primary School, as well as PTA treasurer of the Quarry Nursery School.  He is a passionate advocate for safer streets, green spaces and boosting affordable housing provision.

Kai gets things done. With extensive experience delivering engineering and industrial projects — including wind energy, hydropower, solar, recycling and infrastructure — he focuses on practical solutions that create real benefits for local people. With his scientific and business expertise and a strong commitment to good, fulfilling jobs, fair opportunities, and our community, Kai would be a dedicated and effective councillor.

He says: "It’s time for a change in Q&R!"

Stephen Hurt (he/him) has lived and worked in Oxford for over two decades. He strongly believes that Oxford is an open and welcoming city, but it is also facing increasing inequality – making it clear that we need to put people and the planet at the centre of our politics, and  that only by electing more Greens that this will be possible.

Stephen teaches Politics and International Relations at Oxford Brookes University and is an active member of his trade union branch and the local Trades Council. Outside of work he volunteers as a youth cricket coach at a local club.

Living in the heart of Rose Hill with my partner, and two children who go to school locally, I am rooted in my community, serving residents since 2002. Alongside my council role, I work for a university, and am a referee for semi-professional football. Some of my local achievements include establishing the new community centre, winning on grit bins, sorting fly-tipping issues, co-ordinating community support during the pandemic and volunteering with Rose Hill Youth FC. In this time I am pleased to have been able to work with so many local groups from Rose Hill and Donnington Advice Centre to Rose Hill runners. I am passionate about getting things done and making a difference for Rose Hill and delivering for residents.

As an independent City Council candidate for Rose Hill and Iffley, I’m deeply rooted in this ward where my parents have lived for 40 years.

I’ve been a local resident and entrepreneur running local businesses for over a decade. I understand the economic  pressures faced by residents and local businesses. While I champion sustainability through my work in the EV sector, I believe transport must be practical. I’m passionate about removing LTNs and reviewing road speeds to resolve the severe congestion and keep Oxford’s transport flowing smoothly for everyone.

I also stand for global human rights and as a pro-Palestine voice for the community. I have worked in Local Government for many years . The role of Local Government is to help local people not making life more difficult. My whole family has voted for Labour for many years , Labour have let us down and we need change.

As an independent councillor, my commitment is to listen to your concerns and deliver common-sense leadership and pragmatic solutions for our community.

Luke (he/him) has lived in Oxford for most of his life, and in Rose Hill for the last ten years. He has spent 15 years as a secondary school Physics teacher, and is currently retraining as a psychotherapist. He has previously stood as a City Council Candidate for a different party, but has since joined the Green Party.

He was a member of the Oxford City Council's Inclusive Transport & Movement Focus Group, and has advocated on behalf of other disabled people to ensure transport in Oxford remains accessible to everyone. He enjoys cycling, and is passionate about improving bicycle infrastructure in Oxford.

Ahalya Bala (she/her) is a social sciences lecturer who is committed to principles of social and environmental justice and she has been working and living in Oxford for 6 years. She strives to foster connections between the university and the wider community and has organised events around building solidarity with Palestine, challenging state crimes, and celebrating African heritage through music and dance.

Ahalya has been a long-term member of a community-owned housing cooperative and a renters union in Oxford. She is a committee member of a local community choir and she loves engaging in activities, which help to build community cohesion - you can regularly find her singing, dancing, and practising meditation and yoga in the community.

Ahalya is an active trade union member and believes in supporting and protecting people across the public sector. Like so many others, Ahalya has found hope in the Green Party and wants to work with local Green Party councillors to make Oxford a safe, clean and welcoming home for all who live here.

I have deep roots in Oxford – I grew up here, went to school here, studied at Oxford Brookes, and am now building a career here.

As a School Director, I’ve worked closely with young people and families from around the world. My experience supporting international students has strengthened my belief that Oxford’s diversity is one of its greatest strengths and that everyone, regardless of background, should feel welcome in our great city.

I want to use my experience in education to make a real difference for local people. Oxford should be a city where no one is left behind, where we protect our green spaces, support local businesses, and strengthen the sense of belonging that makes St Clement’s so special.

Pippa Hitchcock is a new recruit to our team who worked in university administration for many years, but is now better known in Headington as the landlady of The Butchers Arms. She has lived in Cowley and Headington since arriving in Oxford 20 years ago, and loves being part of the community.

Emily Kerr (she/her) has lived in St. Mary’s for more than 10 years and was first elected a Green Party Councillor in 2022. She was previously a governor of SSMJ school in the ward, and is also a County Councillor as well as the Active Travel Champion for Oxfordshire County Council.

Emily is a Swedish/English dual national with extensive European business experience, having led eBay’s fashion & home division, consulted for a number of leading companies, and run her own start-up. She is particularly passionate about supporting local businesses and community initiatives, improving buses and making streets safer for walking and cycling.

Toby lived on St Mary’s Road and Fairacres Road for years. He’s living on his narrowboat on the Oxford Canal these days, and he’s passionate about improving housing, transport, and access to nature for everyone in our city.

Toby says: “I am standing for election to Oxford City Council because I want to tackle the climate crisis, campaign for rent controls, and speak up for our community and green spaces.”

“We need to reduce pollution, create safe liveable streets, and have roads, cycle routes, and a public transport system that work for everyone.”

“I’m a trade union organiser and the former President of the Oxford & District Trades Council. This is the approach I want to bring to our local politics. I’ll stand against top-down decisions and work to empower everyone to help make East Oxford and our city the very best it can be.“

I have lived in North Oxford for over 25 years, as a student, worker and parent – my 3 children attended Phil & Jim and Cherwell schools. I am active in the community activities and currently a school governor at two local schools. I have always worked in education. Earlier in my career I was a secondary school teacher, education producer and Oxfordshire School Advisor. I currently teach and research at the Open University, supporting students from diverse backgrounds to achieve their potential and partnering with colleagues across the globe to improve teacher training systems.
Why am I doing this? Because I am committed to our community and want to make Oxford a better, fairer place to live for all. If elected I would listen hard to the views of Summertown residents and champion their interests from a position of local knowledge.

James Hewett (he/him) has called Oxford home since 1990 and has lived in many of the neighbourhoods that make this city so uniquely diverse. For more than 25 years he has worked as a social worker, supporting vulnerable children and their families. He has also volunteered as a grassroots football coach for the past decade. With three teenagers attending local schools, James feels strongly connected to the city and invested in protecting and improving services for all.
 
A member of the Green Party of England and Wales since 2000, James believes it offers a credible alternative to politics as usual. His priorities include protecting Oxford’s green spaces, restoring clean waterways, and tackling road congestion in ways that work for drivers, cyclists and public transport users alike.

Dear Resident,

I have lived in Oxfordshire for many years and am
appalled at its decline under the Liberal
Democrats. That’s why I’m standing as your local
Conservative Party candidate in North Hinksey, at
the Oxfordshire County Council elections in May.

I want to see common sense return to politics.
The Lib Dems have wasted millions on unpopular
road closures and traffic restrictions. This has pulled money away from fixing potholes and providing vital SEND services for the county’s children.

Judy Roberts, the incumbent Lib Dem councillor,
is well-respected locally. But the party she
represents has damaged our county. The Lib
Dems don’t listen to the public. They ignore
consultations and press on with their own
agenda. The only way to change this is by
returning a Conservative majority in May. I
therefore urge you to vote Conservative for real
and lasting change.

Chris Fox

Heather Stallard (she/her) has lived in East Oxford for 3 years. She works in professional services at the University of Oxford, managing communications and events for environmental research. She used to work in the homelessness sector and is particularly passionate and aggravated about the Oxford housing crisis.

After many years of losing faith in the Labour Party, she left to join the Green Party. Heather believes in policies that take care of the least well off, and believes we can do that at the same time as taking care of our planet for animals, nature and future generations.

I am a GP working in the NHS. I have been a local GP for 20 years and care deeply about my patients, the local community and the NHS. I have learned how to solve problems for my patients and I have a huge amount of experience about how the NHS and social care works.

I have also been a city councillor before. I have 10 years experience of fixing problems for residents and I know how to get things done. As a councillor I led the building of new council houses, regulating poor landlords and reducing homelessness. In my wards I successfully campaigned for better play areas, youth schemes and community facilities. If elected I would prioritise improving housing standards, cleaner streets, healthier environments and more public transport.

Tony Brett, a Cowley Resident of 25 years, has served 9 years on Oxford City Council meaning he is experienced in getting things done and representing his electorate effectively.  He says: 
“Living in the heart of Cowley Marsh on Reliance Way I am easily accessible to the community.  I serve as Chair of Governors at Oxford Spires Academy; Coordinator of the Reliance Way area Neighbourhood Watch; and as an active member of the Cowley Community Forum and our NextDoor online group.  I’ve organised many OxClean sessions for our area and have a passion for building inclusive community.  If elected I would tackle drug dealing, dangerous driving, lack of affordable housing, and exorbitant agent fees for tenants.  I would make myself available to as many community events and groups as possible and promise to listen carefully to folk from all parts of our rich and diverse community.”
Professionally Tony is the Oxford University’s Medical Sciences Division IT Services Director at the John Radcliffe Hospital.

I am a neuroscientist, teaching students at Oxford University. I have represented Walton Manor on the City Council for 12 years, and during 8 of those years I have taken on the extra responsibility of Cabinet positions. I am standing for re-election because I want to make Oxford a fairer, better place to live. I am very proud of Labour’s record in administration on tackling inequality: we have the largest council-house building programme in decades; we have kept full exemption from council tax for those on the lowest incomes.
There are many active resident groups in Walton Manor, Norham Manor, Waterside and Park Town, and I keep in touch with them all year round.
Although I was not born in Oxford, I have been here for 30 years and I love it. My year as Lord Mayor has shown me the tremendous strength we have in our diverse communities. People come here from across the globe, and I believe that we must all work together to give everyone the best chance in life.

Zelalemawee Asheber (he/him) is running for Walton Manor because he wants to ensure, by implementing the Green manifesto, that not only does Walton Manor remain a beautiful place to live but becomes more affordable too.

Zelalemawee is currently doing a PhD in antibiotic resistance research at the University of Oxford. He pursued this work because of a long-term motivation to help others that is derived from a gratitude for the selfless care of his mother and an awareness of the current unmet needs of people in this country and abroad.

By working to improve those inadequate conditions, it allows him to do as Sylvia Pankhurst wrote: ‘It came to me as a challenge: seek to obtain for others the care you had.’

For the exact same reason, he wants to work just as hard for his local area, Walton Manor.

Cristina Palamini (she/they) is standing as a Green Party candidate because she believes Oxford has the talent and resources to be a greener, fairer city — and that what’s needed now is the political will to act. She cares deeply about Oxford as a place to live, work and build a future, and believes local politics must be rooted in fairness, sustainability and long-term thinking.

She has lived in Oxford for five years. As an immigrant to the UK, she was drawn by the openness of the community and is committed to keeping Oxford a city where people from all backgrounds can belong and build secure, fulfilling lives.

She has been politically active throughout her life as an activist, motivated by climate action and social justice. As an Italian, she brings a strong awareness of the risks of complacency in the face of extremism and believes Oxford needs bold leadership to address the climate emergency, the housing crisis and the cost of living.

She works as Chief Product Officer at an education technology start-up focused on widening access to learning, bringing a commitment to practical, evidence-based solutions alongside the ambition needed to deliver real change."

Bev has been a trustee at the Agnes Smith Advice Centre down in Blackbird Leys for many years.

Bev worked at Oxfam for 35 years. She is dedicated to fighting injustice, poverty, and inequality across Oxford and beyond. Since her retirement in 2022, Bev has been volunteering as a receptionist and a cashier at the Oxfam charity shop on the Cowley Road.

As a former school governor, Bev is active in her local community and local Labour Party, and she’d encourage others to get involved too.
